data.title: Lonza to sell capsule and health ingredients business to Lone Star for $3.0 billion
data.description:
Lonza will sell its capsules and health ingredients business to private equity firm Lone Star Funds for an enterprise value of 2.3 billion Swiss francs ($3.0 billion), the Swiss contract drug manufacturer said on Friday.

The company has been seeking to exit the business since December 2024, with an aim to focus solely on its core contract drug manufacturing offering.

KEY DETAILS

- Lonza will receive upfront proceeds of 1.7 billion Swiss francs and retain a 40% stake in the business

- Total undiscounted proceeds are expected to be at or above 3 billion Swiss francs

- The transaction is expected to close in the second half of 2026

($1 = 0.7777 Swiss francs)

data.title: EU wheat rallies with crude oil as Mideast war rattles investors
data.description:
Euronext wheat futures climbed to their highest since August on Friday, supported by another surge in oil prices as investors fretted over disruption from the Middle East conflict, traders said.

May milling wheat (BL2K6) on Euronext was up 2.7% at 207.50 euros ($240.66) per metric ton by 1711 GMT.

The contract earlier reached its highest since August 15 at 208.7 euros, surpassing a previous six-month peak from Tuesday.

Grain markets were again spurred by rising oil prices, with Brent crude reaching $90 a barrel for the first time since April 2024.

Comments by Qatar's energy minister, who said if the war continues he expects all Gulf energy producers to shut down exports within weeks and oil prices to reach $150, further unsettled investors.

Wheat can be sensitive to crude oil, partly due to commodity positions taken by investment funds and also because other grains like corn are widely used for making biofuel.

"You've got flows from commodity funds and you also have the indirect link with corn," a futures dealer said of wheat's rise.

The run-up to the weekend was also encouraging short-covering by participants, like last Friday when expectations grew of a U.S.-Iran war, traders said.

On the physical market, traders said wheat buyers appeared mostly calm against a backdrop of ample global availability.

"In its wheat tender today, Tunisia even bought 25,000 tons less than the volume it sought offers for," one trader said.

There was nonetheless some more demand from Egypt, which traders regarded as war-related short-covering.

An Egyptian buyer was seeking about 30,000 tons of Black Sea 11.5% protein wheat at around $253 a ton cost and freight included (c&f) for April shipment, another trader said.

Grain importers in Arab Gulf countries faced logistical snags from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Some were seeking alternative ports to unload, including on the UAE's east coast and in Oman, while Saudi Gulf wheat shipments were being transferred to Red Sea ports, traders said.

“Some blocked shipments waiting outside the Hormuz strait are being offered for resale. This generally involves corn for Iran although some wheat is also seeking new buyers,” the second trader said.

($1 = 0.8622 euros)

data.title: Gasoline refining margins slide on stronger crude
data.description:
Northwest European gasoline refinery profit margins fell sharply by about $5 to about $3 a barrel on Friday, amid a surge in underlying crude prices.

  • About 14,000 metric tons of E5 gasoline barges traded in the Argus window, with ExxonMobil and Sahara selling to TotalEnergies, Gunvor, Varo and MB Energy.

  • Another 6,000 tons of E10 gasoline barges changed hands, as Exxon and Trafigura sold to Varo and MB Energy.

  • Platts raised the incrementability for European naphtha and gasoline to ±$2/mt every 20 seconds, replacing the prior ±$1/mt limits, the price reporting agency said on Friday.

  • The changes reflect heightened market volatility driven by vessel transit disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz, it added.

  • Gasoline stocks held independently in the Amsterdam-Rotterdam-Antwerp (ARA) refining and storage hub rose by about 6% on the week, data from Dutch consultancy Insights Global showed on Thursday.

  • Gasoline stocks stood at 1.4 million metric tons.

  • Several refineries in the Middle East, including in Kuwait, Qatar and Bahrain, were experiencing shutdowns as a result of the conflict in the region.

data.title: Bulls, bears back down; neutral stock sentiment steps forward - AAII
data.description:
Key points:
  • All three major U.S. stock sharply lower; Dow off most
  • All S&P 500 sectors red; Financials down most
  • European shares down ~1%
  • US oil surges nearly 11%; gold up >1%; dollar reverses, now lower; bitcoin down >3.5%
  • US 10-yr Treasury yield edged down to 4.13%

BULLS, BEARS BACK DOWN; NEUTRAL STOCK SENTIMENT STEPS FORWARD - AAII

The number of individual investors who see no short-term change in the stock market is on the rise, according to the latest weekly survey from the American Association of Individual Investors (AAII).

Bullish sentiment, or the belief that stock prices will increase over the next six months, edged 0.1 percentage point lower to 33.1%, riding below its long-term historical average of 37.5% for the third time in 14 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, or the conviction that stock prices will soften over the next six months, shed 4.2 pps to 35.5%, but remained above its 31.0% historical average for the fourth straight week.

But neutral sentiment, or those who expect the stock market to move sideways in the near-term, jumped 4.4 pps to 31.4%, hovering just below its 31.5% historical average, where it's been for 85 of the last 87 weeks.

Combined, the bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) widened by 4.1 ppts to -2.5. That's the fourth straight week that the spread has been narrower than the 6.5 historical average.

Last week, AAII asked its survey participants to weigh in on their perception of inflation.

Most (38.6%) said it's slowing, but not by enough, while 33.2% replied that price growth is returning to a more acceptable pace. Still, 22.4% believe that inflation is still rising too quickly.

The balance were unsure, or had no opinion.

data.title: Oil Prices Soar Past $90. Kuwait Cut Production — and Trump Wants 'Unconditional Surrender' From Iran. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Laura Sanicola and Alex Kozul-Wright

The price of Brent crude just surged past $90 a barrel for the first time in two years as the escalating conflict in Iran continues to fuel a meteoric rally that shows no signs of slowing down. The last time the global benchmark cleared the psychologically important $90 level was April 16, 2024.

Brent crude futures were up 5.4% to $90.11 a barrel in Friday trading. West Texas Intermediate futures were up 7.6% at $87.33.

The move came after Kuwait was forced to reduce production at some of its oil facilities, according to a report from The Wall Street Journal. The country is suffering storage issues as Iran has effectively shut down the Strait of Hormuz, the main way to ship oil out of the Persian Gulf.

Kuwait, a member of OPEC, produces 2.6 million barrels a day of oil, according to the cartel's official data. The country is looking to produce only what it can consume domestically, according to the Journal report, which is about 1.2 million barrels a day, according to an analysis from OPIS, a Dow Jones company.

Kuwait would be the latest Middle East oil producer to cut production due to a lack of available vessels to carry crude. And once local storage availability fills up, production cuts are inevitable.

Iraq has already had to cut production by about half, and producers in Saudi Arabia and the U.A.E. are running out of storage as well, according to oil intelligence firm Kpler.

In an interview published Friday morning, Qatar's Energy Minister Saad al-Kaabi told the Financial Times that war in the Middle East could force Persian Gulf countries to halt energy production within days. That could drive oil up to $150 a barrel, he warned.

Energy consultancy Wood Mackenzie said earlier this week that oil prices could exceed] $100 if the flow of tankers through the Strait isn't quickly restored.

The rise in oil prices over the past week has been nothing short of dramatic. Both benchmarks have now rallied over the past five trading days. Brent crude is up 19% in that period, while WTI has risen by an eye-popping 25% — their biggest weekly gains since the week ending March 4, 2022, at the start of Russia's war in Ukraine.

In addition to fears about oil supply drying up, President Donald Trump ratcheted up rhetoric about his goal in the Middle East. On Friday morning, he said that "there will be no deal with Iran except UNCONDITIONAL SURRENDER!," adding that an end would include "the selection of a GREAT & ACCEPTABLE Leader(s)."

The administration has repeatedly said the war would last several weeks and that it would be temporary.

When crude prices rise, shares of oil companies tend to get a boost. That is why, despite a 1.3% drop in the S&P 500 on Friday, the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR exchange-traded fund was flat. Its two biggest holdings — Exxon Mobil and Chevron — were also holding up better than the broader market. The United States Oil Fund, an ETF that tracks daily price movements of WTI, was up nearly 10%.

Write to Laura Sanicola at laura.sanicola@barrons.com and Alex Kozul-Wright at alexander.kozul-wright@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: CBP Says Tariff Refund System Will Be Operational in 45 Days
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

U.S. Customs and Border Protection says a system for tariff refunds will be operational in 45 days following the Supreme Court's ruling striking down President Trump's sweeping global tariffs last month.

The agency said it will work on a streamlined system for refund payments rather than manually processing individual tariff requests, which it said in a Friday court filing would be unfeasible given the agency's capacity.

The filing comes in response to a Thursday ruling in the Court of International Trade requiring the agency to begin refunding upwards of $130 billion in tariff payments collected under the previous tariff regime.

The agency said the refund process will be carried out on an importer basis, rather than issuing refunds for more than 53 million separate tariff payment entries.

"The process will be simpler and more efficient than the existing functionalities, and CBP will provide guidance on how to file refund declarations in the new system," the agency said in the filing.

CBP said in the filing it had received "an unprecedented volume of refunds." Companies including Costco and FedEx have filed lawsuits requesting to be reimbursed for their tariff payments.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: Drone Makers Expect Surging Demand But Face Scaling Challenges — Analysis
data.description:
By Nicholas G. Miller

Drone manufacturers are set to see a surge in business as the conflict in the Middle East highlights the fundamental role drones play in modern fighting. But analysts say those companies face challenges in ramping up production and risk of losing to competitors in a fragmented, unpredictable market.

The damage cheap, low-flying Iranian drones have dealt the U.S. and its allies this week has underscored the military's need to dramatically increase its supply of drones and counter-drone technology, analysts say.

While the Russia-Ukraine war has long demonstrated the growing importance of drones, the conflict with Iran "serves as a real accelerant, or shot in the arm for the whole trend," Stifel analyst Jonathan Siegmann said.

Drone companies are likely to see a glut of new orders in coming months, but they will have to prove that they can ramp up production fast and fend off competitors' technology, analysts say.

Leaders in the drone market include Kratos, which makes the fighter-jet-like Valkyrie drone, AeroVironment, a producer known for its Switchblade kamikaze drone, and Ondas, which is seeing rising demand for its counter-drone technology.

These companies have benefited from taking risks in the development of drone technology that the largest military contractors have been unwilling to assume, analysts said.

Leading military contractors are "not willing to invest ahead of need," BTIG analyst Andre Madrid said. "They don't want to invest the upfront capital to develop this technology...they're going to wait until there's a government contract in their face with their name on it."

Additionally, some of the key military advantages of drones are their low-cost and simplicity, which allows smaller firms to compete. "The defense primes are used to working on major defense acquisition programs with very large, expensive, exquisite systems," Canaccord Genuity analyst Austin Moeller said. "The problem is drones are the polar opposite of that."

The Pentagon under President Trump is seeking to increase competition, boost mid-level contractors and reward companies that invest, analysts said. It is also awarding contracts and placing orders much faster than before.

As a result, drone companies are likely to see lucrative orders in the coming weeks, rather than years, analysts said.

To meet a surge in demand drone makers will have to rapidly increase manufacturing capacity, which is a challenge, especially for smaller companies. The government also wants all of the key components produced in the U.S., which makes scaling up production even harder, said Needham analyst Austin Bohlig.

"There's a lot of money flowing around, but if you don't have the infrastructure in place that allows you to execute on those awards, it makes it very difficult," Madrid said.

There are several companies that have innovative drone technologies but are likely to struggle to win and deliver on contracts due to scaling challenges, analysts say.

"We're definitely dealing with a Pentagon that does not want great to be the enemy of good and would rather take a solution today than a great solution five years from now," Madrid said. He added that companies are starting to form more strategic partnerships with larger contractors to help scale their drone production.

The necessary investments in expanding capacity can be risky, especially in a fragmented market with technology that is rapidly changing.

The Pentagon is distributing orders between companies, which makes it difficult for them to generate consistent revenue and plan ahead, said Citizens analyst Trevor Walsh.

"Having the right product today doesn't necessarily mean you're gonna have the right product for next week because things are changing so much. This isn't the old defense contractor model where if you win something today you're gonna be making it for decades," Stifel's Siegmann said. "There's gonna be churn in these programs and it's a really fragmented group of companies…so there's gonna be some losers."

Write to Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com.

data.title: BlackRock Sticks to Redemption Minimum on Credit Fund, Sends Shares Lower — WSJ
data.description:
By Jack Pitcher

BlackRock limited withdrawals for the first time from one of its flagship private-credit funds, marking the latest sign of investor discomfort with the health of the debt markets.

Shares of BlackRock, the world's largest asset manager, fell 6% in late morning trading to the lowest level since May.

The HPS Corporate Lending Fund, a business-development company that doesn't trade on an exchange, said Friday it would stick to its plan to buy back up to 5% of the fund's shares, the minimum amount it agreed to in a given quarter.

The decision mattered this quarter because the fund, known by its ticker, Hlend, had received requests to redeem 9.3% of its shares, according to a letter BlackRock sent to investors on Friday. It was the first quarter in the fund's four-year history in which withdrawal requests exceeded 5%.

BlackRock's unwillingness to go beyond the fund's stated minimum redemptions is a departure from the path chosen by one of its biggest rivals, Blackstone. On Monday, Blackstone said it had received record withdrawal requests from its massive $82 billion fund known as Bcred, and would fulfill all of them, representing 7.9% of shares.

To meet those requests, a number of senior Blackstone employees invested more than $150 million in the fund, Bloomberg News reported. Blackstone shares fell on the news anyway.

Hlend, like Bcred, is marketed to wealthy individual investors and manages a portfolio of loans to midsize companies.

HPS's Hlend has generated a 10.7% annualized return net of fees since its inception, and placing limits on liquidity is key to its strategy, the fund's managers said in Friday's letter to investors.

"Hlend's intentionally designed liquidity framework, specifically the recurring 5% quarterly share repurchase feature, is foundational to enabling these return outcomes," the letter said. "Without it, there would be a structural mismatch between investor capital and the expected duration of the private credit loans in which Hlend invests."

After years of record fundraising and at times having to turn investors away from funds, private credit managers are now dealing with a rapid change in sentiment. A number of high-profile defaults have raised questions about credit quality in the fast-growing industry, which often offers higher yields than the public bond and loan markets in exchange for less liquidity.

Private credit managers make large loans directly to companies, bypassing the traditional syndicated bond market run by banks. Their biggest clients are pensions and insurance funds, but wealthy individuals have also flocked to the asset class in recent years, often through "semiliquid" funds that allow capped investments and withdrawals at specific intervals.

For the first time since the start of the private credit boom, large numbers of individual investors are trying to get out, putting stress on managers.

BlackRock, which spun out of Blackstone in the 1990s, became the world's largest asset manager primarily through managing public stock and bond funds and a focus on indexed investing. A yearslong push to beef up its private asset businesses, which generate higher fees, led it to buy HPS Investment Partners, one of the largest private credit managers, in a deal that closed last year.
